Topic 5: Part 1 (the constant cycle, no objectives & further escalation)

[183:24]Where to even begin? It's hard to say what is even happening here. We have effectively

[183:31]been in a stalemate with Iran for the past eight weeks, and nobody really knows what

[183:39]is happening.

[183:40]What we are being told is that we are in a ceasefire with Iran because we are working

[183:48]We are engaging Iran in diplomacy. There are bilateral negotiations between the United

[183:54]States and Iran. They're actually being mediated. They're indirect through Pakistan and Qatar.

[184:00]However, it's hard to say if that is even true. This is what we are being told. And yet

[184:07]at the same time, it seems that every week there is this familiar cycle where the administration

[184:16]And they say on truth social or in the press pool or in the Oval Office, they will tell

[184:22]us that Iran is begging us to make a deal.

[184:26]They have begged us for an extension of the ceasefire.

[184:29]They want to make a deal really badly.

[184:32]They're making major concessions and that a deal is imminent.

[184:37]We have probably heard this no fewer than a dozen times since the war started.

[184:43]And then what inevitably happens is that within 24 to 48 hours, the Iranian side will come

[184:49]out publicly and say that everything the administration is saying is a lie.

[184:55]And Iran will come out and say, we are not talking to the United States.

[184:59]We don't want to talk to the United States.

[185:03]We don't want to make a deal.

[185:05]We can wait the United States out.

[185:07]We have all the time in the world.

[185:09]And we're not making any concessions.

[185:11]These are our demands and they haven't changed at all.

[185:15]That's the second part.

[185:17]And then within days of this, the United States comes out,

[185:21]Trump in particular will say that he is finally done messing around

[185:25]and the Iranians are not taking him seriously.

[185:28]And they're crazy and they don't know what they're doing.

[185:31]And if they don't get their act together, then we're going to bomb them again.

[185:35]And this is the cycle. We have seen this basically every week for the past eight to nine weeks.

[185:42]They want to make a deal. They're making concessions. A deal is imminent. We are, we are planning

[185:48]all these meetings. We're going to Pakistan. We're doing this and that. Iran says none

[185:53]of that is true. Our demands haven't changed. It's still the same. And then Trump comes

[185:58]out and says, we're going to bomb them. And then it starts all over again the next week.

[186:02]It's like we all just forget.

[186:04]It starts all over again the next week, we don't bomb them.

[186:07]Trump comes out and says, oh, we don't need to bomb them because they beg for a ceasefire

[186:11]and they want a deal more than anyone knows and they're making concessions.

[186:15]They call us liars, he threatens to bomb.

[186:17]This has been the cycle and it's hard to know exactly what the strategy is or what even

[186:23]the objectives are.

[186:25]Remember the original objectives in the conflict were announced by Trump the day that the war

[186:32]We were seeking the destruction of Iran's stockpile of highly enriched uranium.

[186:38]We wanted to destroy their enrichment capability.

[186:42]We wanted to degrade their stockpile of missiles and drones.

[186:46]We wanted to stop them from supporting regional proxies.

[186:49]We wanted to sink their Navy and institute a regime change.

[186:59]We have not destroyed their highly enriched uranium.

[187:02]We've not destroyed their nuclear capability, and they're not willing to give up either

[187:06]of those things.

[187:07]We have not destroyed their stockpile of drones and missiles.

[187:10]We haven't even significantly degraded them.

[187:13]They're actually rebuilding what they've lost, and that will be completed within six months.

[187:18]We failed that regime change.

[187:19]If anything, the regime is now stronger.

[187:23]At the minimum, it is more resilient,

[187:25]it is more defiant, and it is dug in.

[187:28]It's not going anywhere anytime soon.

[187:34]then have we abandoned them?

[187:37]And if we haven't abandoned them,

[187:38]then how are we going to achieve them in the future

[190:07]Well, it seems that in the first few weeks the administration was looking for a creative

[193:38]This was the next form of denial.

[193:47]So then they came up with this harebrained idea.

[193:55]to Iranian shipping.

[193:58]Because how it has worked so far is that Iran's IRGC is closing shipping for everybody

[194:07]So Iran still has sea-based shipping at all of their ports going through the straight.

[194:14]Obviously, they're not going to target their own ships with mines and drones and missiles.

Topic 6: Part 2 (past & current deals, Lebanon's role, and Israel's sabotage)

[195:48]And again, they're promoting this idea of peace talks

[195:51]When in reality, we just have no cards to play. We have no leverage and let's talk about the negotiations as they stand

[196:01]Where the negotiations stand right now between the US and Iran. It's fundamentally unchanged

[196:07]From the beginning of the war from before the war

[196:13]The United States is demanding the same set of things.

[196:17]We are telling Iran we want them to give up

[196:19]their highly enriched uranium.

[196:21]We want them to stop enriching uranium altogether.

[196:25]And we're telling them we want the Strait of Hormuz opened

[196:30]in such a way where commercial shipping

[196:32]does not have to pay a toll to Iran.

[196:34]They want freedom of navigation in the Strait.

[196:37]And this is what we are demanding.

[196:39]On the other side, Iran is demanding basically the opposite.

[196:43]Iran says, we will never give up our highly enriched uranium.

[218:39]We have to understand the fundamental strategic objectives of each party involved.

[218:47]Let's think about this. What does Israel want? Simple question, simple answer.

[218:54]What Israel is after, has been after for 50 years since the ascendance of the Likud party

[219:02]in the mid 1970s is a greater Israel.

[219:07]This is what defines Israel's grand strategy.

[219:11]This is their geostrategic doctrine in the Middle East.

[219:16]It is this, they say we are through with exchanging land for peace.

[219:23]We're not giving up territory to make peace with our neighbors.

[219:26]They say rather territory is the most valuable thing we have Israel.

[219:36]They lament that Israel surrendered the Sinai Peninsula to Egypt in 1979 as part of the

[219:44]Camp David Accords. Why? Because the Sinai had energy, something Israel does not have a lot of.

[219:51]And Israel laments that they returned the West Bank and Gaza to the Palestinians.

[219:57]They say land is something we don't have a lot of.

[220:01]We need to have as much of it as we can get

[220:03]because it means security and it means resources

[220:07]and it means living space.

[220:12]what you need to understand is they seek the annexation
